attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for routine follow-up of her bronchiectasis. She reports feeling well with her usual daily productive cough of small amounts of clear to white sputum. She has not experienced any recent exacerbations or changes in her symptoms. She has a medical history of bronchiectasis diagnosed five years ago following recurrent chest infections. She does not take any regular medications. She is a non-smoker and works as a librarian.

On examination, she appears well with pulse rate 72/min, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Chest auscultation reveals scattered coarse crackles at both lung bases. A recent sputum culture reveals Haemophilus influenzae with no previous cultures available for comparison.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Written action plan for antibiotic use during exacerbations
attributes.explanation:
This patient has stable bronchiectasis with colonisation by Haemophilus influenzae. In the absence of acute exacerbation symptoms (increased sputum volume, change in sputum colour, fever, or increased dyspnoea), bacterial colonisation does not require antibiotic treatment. The most appropriate management is patient education and self-management strategies.

Written action plan for antibiotic use during exacerbations is the correct approach. These plans empower patients to recognise early signs of exacerbation and initiate prompt antibiotic therapy, which has been shown to reduce hospitalisations and improve quality of life. The plan should include clear criteria for starting antibiotics and which antibiotic to use based on previous sputum cultures.

Oral amoxicillin for 14 days would be appropriate for an acute exacerbation caused by H. influenzae, with the standard duration being 14 days rather than seven days. However, this is not indicated in stable disease. Treating colonisation without symptoms can promote antibiotic resistance and does not improve outcomes.

Inhaled salbutamol twice daily is not routinely recommended in bronchiectasis unless there is demonstrable reversible airway obstruction or coexistent asthma. Bronchodilators do not improve outcomes in most patients with bronchiectasis.

Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccination protects against invasive disease caused by encapsulated H. influenzae type b, not the non-typeable strains that commonly colonise the airways in bronchiectasis. Australian/New Zealand and international guidelines do not recommend H. influenzae vaccination for bronchiectasis patients. However, they do recommend annual influenza vaccination, pneumococcal vaccination (both 13-valent conjugate and 23-valent polysaccharide vaccines), and ensuring routine immunisations are up to date. Come international guidelines do reccommend H. influenzae vaccination in individuals with underlying immunodeficiencies, although such a deficiency has not been demonstrated in this case.

Long-term azithromycin three times weekly may be considered in patients with frequent exacerbations (three or more per year) despite optimal management. This patient appears stable without recent exacerbations, so macrolide prophylaxis is not currently indicated.
learningPoints: Stable bronchiectasis patients with bacterial colonisation do not require antibiotic treatment unless experiencing an exacerbation
Patient education with written action plans for antibiotic use during exacerbations reduces hospitalisations and improves quality of life
Haemophilus influenzae is a common colonising organism in bronchiectasis that does not require treatment in the absence of acute symptoms
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023 Apr;28(4):339–49.
Hill A.T., Sullivan A.L., Chalmers J.D., De Soyza A., Stuart Elborn J., Andres Floto R., et al. British thoracic society guideline for bronchiectasis in adults. Thorax. 2019;74(Supplement 1):74.
Polverino E, Goeminne PC, McDonnell MJ, Aliberti S, Marshall SE, Loebinger MR, et al. European Respiratory Society guidelines for the management of adult bronchiectasis. Eur Respir J. 2017;50(3).
